body_txt: Subtrope of Meaningful Name and Punny Name . So this one's pretty simple: Doing so is a pretty easy way of sneaking in a joke that will go over children's heads because a person can simply say, " Thanks , Dick! " and emphasize it in a specific manner that allows the adults in the audience to understand that this character is not addressing the person exclusively by name. Please note that this trope doesn't apply to ALL characters of note named Richard or Dick. Basic rule of thumb is, if they manage to cram puns on their name into dialogue, the author most likely intended this trope to be used. As "Dick" as a nickname is less common than it once was, this often overlaps with Baby Name Trend Killer and Outdated Name . If used as a Double Entendre , then it falls under Euphemistic Names . For those curious about where this name came from and how it's related to penises, the nickname originates from the old tradition of rhyming nicknames. Robert —> Rob, Bob, Hob, Dob. Margaret —> Meg, Peg, Molly, Polly. Rickard —> Rick, Hick, Dick. Yes, the term "hick" comes from the fact that only a seriously out-of-date country jay would be so old-fashioned as to still go by "Hick". As for penises, at various times, some names have been so common that they became synonymous with the everyman. "Jack" was so common that it became a generic masculine, hence "jackass" for a male ass. "Dick" was common enough (think "every Tom, Dick, and Harry") that it faced this, and for a time in the 17th century became slang for a male sexual partner. Hence, through synecdoche , went from referring to the whole man to just his penis. It eventually came to mean "jerk" in The '60s , probably related to the offensive nature of talking about genitalia generally. "Dick" is also slang for a detective, usually a Private Detective , and the name can be used to make an additional pun with characters who are detectives. There is a minor Chinese-based word that can count as this: Wang. Originally, "Wang" is meant to be a glorious reference, as it means "King". However, the English language has a dick-related term that sounds phonetically similar: "wank". Therefore, in English-based audiences, a Chinese character named "Wang" may be subjected to this trope when they fulfill all the traits of the Dick-named jerks/bad guys, as the audience may think that 'wanking' is involved.
